retrieved_docs.title: Cancer Screening -- Function -- Breast Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: According to ACS guidelines published in 2007, annual mammography with MRI is recommended for women at increased risk of breast cancer. This group includes women with a known BRCA mutation, as well as those with a first-degree relative with a BRCA mutation. Additionally, women with a 20% to 25% or higher lifetime risk of breast cancer, as predicted by breast cancer risk-estimation models, are included. Screening protocols would include pedigree analysis of first- and second-degree relatives on both paternal and maternal sides, individuals treated for Hodgkin disease with chest irradiation before age 30, women with Cowden, Li-Fraumeni, and Bannayan-Riley-Ruvalcaba syndromes, and the first-degree relatives of all these patients. The ACS guideline for women at increased risk is currently being updated.

retrieved_docs.title: BRCA1 and BRCA2 Mutations -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Studies do show that women who carry BRCA mutations are more likely to develop secondary cancer - either in the same breast or the contralateral breast. For these women, prophylactic bilateral mastectomy is recommended. More important, studies show that women who do undergo surgery are less likely to die from breast cancer compared to women who were treated with unilateral mastectomy.

retrieved_docs.title: BRCA1 and BRCA2 Mutations -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutation experience worse breast cancer-specific survival when compared to BRCA- negative patients. BRCA1 carriers have a worse overall survival than BRCA2 patients [11] .

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Cancer Screening in the Average-Risk Patient -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: An early age of initiation for screening and the use of MRI and ultrasound may be considered for women with first-degree relatives diagnosed with breast cancer. [5] For BRCA mutation carriers, a combination of annual breast MRI and mammography is recommended for breast cancer screening. It is essential to accurately identify patients with a greater than 20% lifetime risk of breast cancer and to recognize that standard screening guidelines for asymptomatic average-risk patients do not apply to this population. High-risk individuals should be referred to a breast cancer specialist for tailored screening recommendations and, if appropriate, to a genetic counselor for genetic testing.

retrieved_docs.title: BRCA1 and BRCA2 Mutations -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: A positive BRCA mutation indicates a higher likelihood of developing cancer but does not make or confirm the diagnosis of cancer. Subsequently, a negative BRCA test does not eliminate the risk of developing breast cancer from sporadic or other genetic causes.

retrieved_docs.title: Disparity in Early Detection of Breast Cancer  -- Issues of Concern -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: The autosomal dominant BRCA1 and BRCA2 gene mutations are located on chromosomes 17 and 13, respectively. They produce tumor suppressor proteins that help repair damaged deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA). When these genes do not function properly, DNA damage is not adequately repaired, and cells are more likely to undergo genetic alterations leading to cancer development. The prevalence of these mutations varies by ethnicity. The highest rates of BRCA 1 mutations occur among Ashkenazi Jewish women (8.3%), followed by Hispanic women (3.5%), non-Hispanic White women (2.2%), Black women (1.3%), and Asian women (0.5%). Over half of the women who inherit a BRCA mutation will develop breast cancer by the age of 70. Moreover, women with BRCA mutations are more likely to develop secondary cancers such as ovarian cancer or contralateral breast cancer. They are also more likely to develop "triple-negative" cancer, which has a poorer prognosis than other breast cancers. Therefore, it is recommended that women with early-onset breast cancer or women with a family history consistent with a possible BRCA mutation have genetic testing when breast cancer is diagnosed. As gene sequencing continues to advance, other mutations increasing the risk for breast cancer have been identified. Some additional common gene mutations include PTEN, TP53, MLH1, MLH2, STK11, ATM, CDH1, CHEK2, and PALB2, which should be considered for inclusion in genetic testing in appropriate cases. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Disparity in Early Detection of Breast Cancer  -- Issues of Concern -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: A family history of breast cancer is one of the most widely known risk factors for developing the disease. Women with a family history of breast cancer are nearly 11 times more likely to develop the disease. That risk increases even further when inheritable gene mutations exist. Roughly 10% of breast cancers are hereditary. Of those, nearly half are due to BRCA1 (breast cancer gene 1) and BRCA2 (breast cancer gene 2) gene mutations. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Cancer -- Etiology -- Breast Cancer Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Family history and genetic mutations : First-degree relatives of patients with breast cancer have a 2-fold to 3-fold excess risk for the development of the disease. Genetic factors cause 5% to 10% of all breast cancer cases but may account for 25% of cases in women younger than 30 years. BRCA1 and BRCA2 are the most important genes responsible for increased breast cancer susceptibility.

retrieved_docs.title: Male Breast Cancer -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: When discussing the etiology of breast cancer in. men, one must uncover the potential genetic or environmental risk factors. Being mindful that the majority of males diagnosed with MBC have no identifiable risk other than increasing age (average age of diagnosis of 71) is important. [2] Similar to women, men have a higher risk of breast cancer if they have a first- or second-degree relative with breast cancer. Studies have shown that an affected sibling or parent of either gender may increase the risk of breast cancer in either the males or females in the family. Bevier et al (2012) noted in their study that the relative risk (RR) of breast cancer was similar in either offspring when the father or mother was affected (RR= 1.73 and 1.74, respectively), but the risk was slightly higher in the females when the brother is affected as opposed to when the sister is affected (RR= 2.48 and 1.39, respectively). [3] Also, in addition to a family history of breast cancer, males with a BRCA mutation appear to have an increased risk for breast cancer as well. Although it is rare for a male to have a BRCA mutation, those with a BRCA2 carrier mutation have a 6% increased risk, and BRCA1 has a 4% risk of developing the disease. [4] Other genetic disorders have been implicated in increasing the risk of MBC, including Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY), Cowden syndrome ( PTEN tumor suppressor gene), Li-Fraumeni syndrome ( TP53 ), Lynch syndrome ( PALB2 and mismatch repair genes). [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Cancer Screening in the Average-Risk Patient -- Indications -- The American Society of Breast Surgeons Screening Recommendations
retrieved_docs.content: Women at higher-than-average risk of breast cancer due to hereditary susceptibility from pathogenic mutation carrier status or prior chest wall radiation between the ages of 10 and 30 should begin yearly screening mammography at age 30 and be offered annual supplemental MRI starting at age 25. Additionally, women with a higher-than-average risk of breast cancer due to a predicted lifetime risk greater than 20% from any model or a strong family history should undergo yearly screening mammography and be offered annual supplemental MRI starting at age 35, as recommended by their clinician.

retrieved_docs.title: BRCA1 and BRCA2 Mutations -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Testing patients who have not been diagnosed with cancer is typically reserved for situations when the affected family member or members cannot be tested. Criteria for testing patients without a personal history of breast cancer but with one or more of the following (from the NCCN and American Society of Breast Surgeons Consensus Guideline on Hereditary Genetic Testing) [5] : A first-degree or second-degree relative with age onset of breast cancer less than or equal to 45 Ashkenazi Jewish heritage and family history of breast cancer at any age Two or more primary breast cancer (either asynchronous, synchronous, bilateral, or multicentric) in a single family member Two or more relatives on the same side of the family with breast and/or pancreatic cancer Family or personal history of ovarian, fallopian, or primary peritoneal cancer Male breast cancer Known mutation carrier in the family

retrieved_docs.title: Cowden Disease -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Prognosis is fair, and if cancers are recognized early on in the process, then patients may live close to normal life spans. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Predictive Medicine -- Issues of Concern -- Predictive Medicine
retrieved_docs.content: Ideally, we would calculate a person’s risk for breast cancer or any other disease and intervene appropriately. This is the goal of predictive medicine: obtaining and cataloging characteristics about individual patients, analyzing that data to predict the patient’s risk for an outcome of interest, predicting which treatment might be most effective for which individual, and then intervening before the outcome occurs.Traditionally, predictive medicine was exclusively confined to the realm of genetics. It was once thought that genetics would revolutionize medicine, and genetics and genomics have improved our ability to predict individual risk for some diseases (eg, the BRCA gene and breast cancer) and predict which treatment will be more effective in a given individual (eg, therapy directed at a molecular target in cancer). However, genetics-based risk prediction has proven to be of limited benefit for reasons including (1) whole-genome sequencing is still relatively expensive and not currently covered by insurance, and (2) the majority of diseases afflicting the population today is multifactorial. For example, heart disease is influenced not only by genetics but also by age, diet, exercise, and stress levels. Collecting and organizing this data for analysis is invaluable.

retrieved_docs.title: BRCA1 and BRCA2 Mutations -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Finally, when a patient is identified with BRCA mutations, the team should hold a conference with the patient. The patient should be provided with all the latest information and choices of treatment. The patient's decision should be voluntary and without any due pressure from the clinicians to produce the best outcomes. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: BRCA1 and BRCA2 Mutations -- Treatment / Management -- Surveillance for Patients with BRCA1 and BRCA2 Mutations
retrieved_docs.content: An increased level of monitoring is mandatory for all patients with known BRCA mutations. The NCCN guidelines are widely accepted in the management of patients with BRCA mutation. The main goal of monitoring is early detection of malignancy and high-risk premalignant lesions. Early detection begins with breast awareness and self-breast examination beginning at age 18 and annual or semiannual clinical breast examination at age 25 (though neither of these has shown to benefit survival) [5] .

retrieved_docs.title: BRCA1 and BRCA2 Mutations -- Treatment / Management -- Surveillance for Patients with BRCA1 and BRCA2 Mutations
retrieved_docs.content: Breast MRI increases sensitivity from approximately 33% to approximately 80% sensitivity in the detection of malignancy in patients with a familial or hereditary predisposition and has proven especially useful in this younger subset of patients. According to the NCCN guidelines, annual screening breast MRI with contrast is recommended annually from age 25 to 29 if available, or mammogram annually, if not. From age 30 to 75, annual mammogram and MRI of the breast with contrast is performed [5] .

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2195
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast serves as the anatomical site where breast cancer manifests, with “cancer of breast” identified as a finding located in this tissue. A specific manifestation of this disease is the primary malignant neoplasm of the areola of the female breast, which is classified as a type of breast cancer. Therapeutic management of breast cancer frequently involves breast‑conserving surgical procedures performed on the female breast, such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy, which are directly related to the organ and aim to remove malignant tissue while preserving as much normal breast tissue as possible. This interconnected knowledge underscores the relationship between the site (female breast), the disease (cancer of breast and its areolar subtype), and the standard surgical interventions (lumpectomy; quadrantectomy of breast) used in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2703
retrieved_docs.content: The statement “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” is an instance that signifies the presence of سرطان الثدي (breast cancer). Breast cancer can extend beyond the breast and may appear as cancer of ovary, which is described as a manifestation of سرطان الثدي. The entity cancer of ovary itself can have a subordinate concept نقائل إلى المبيض (metastasis to the ovary), indicating that ovarian lesions may represent metastatic disease. Thus, a malignant breast biopsy confirms breast cancer, which can metastasize to the ovary, and ovarian involvement may be either a primary ovarian cancer or a secondary manifestation of breast cancer, often accompanied by ovarian metastases. Recognizing these interconnections is essential for accurate staging, prognosis, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: اللمفاوية الجذري) are specific modalities within the same therapeutic hierarchy. All mastectomy variants are members of both the malignant breast tumor SMQ and the set of therapeutic and diagnostic procedures for malignant diseases, and they are linked by the “completely_excised_anatomy_has_procedure” relationship to the anatomical structures they remove. Overall, the knowledge network connects diagnostic biopsies of the areola and nipple, the role of these breast parts as sites for cancer detection, and a hierarchical spectrum of surgical interventions—from targeted excisions of accessory or aberrant tissue to partial and total mastectomy with lymph‑node removal—under a unified framework of therapeutic procedures for malignant breast disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 257
retrieved_docs.content: importance of assessing metastatic potential even when the primary lesion’s benign versus malignant nature is unclear. Collectively, these interrelated entities form a hierarchical and functional network that connects genetic syndromes, primary gastrointestinal cancers, various metastatic malignancies, and skin melanomas, providing essential context for accurate diagnosis, staging, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1217
retrieved_docs.content: The statement “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” is identified as a خزعة الثدي (breast biopsy). This indicates that a tissue sample taken from the breast has revealed a malignant tumor, confirming the presence of breast cancer. Recognizing this finding as a type of breast biopsy is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of an appropriate treatment plan.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 896
retrieved_docs.content: The observation that a biopsy of the breast shows a malignant neoplasm is a specific instance of سرطان الثدي (breast cancer). This relationship highlights that histopathologic confirmation of malignant cells in breast tissue directly defines the presence of breast cancer, which is critical for accurate diagnosis and subsequent treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2193
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” is a specific outcome of a breast biopsy (خزعة الثدي) and serves as a diagnostic indicator of breast cancer (سرطان الثدي). In other words, when a breast biopsy reveals a malignant neoplasm, it confirms the presence of breast cancer. Moreover, “breast biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on breast” (خزعة الثدي) represents the broader category of breast tissue sampling and related diagnostic techniques, of which the malignant‑neoplasm finding is one particular result. This relationship highlights how breast biopsy procedures are used to detect malignant disease and how the detection of a malignant neoplasm directly links the procedure to a diagnosis of breast cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1927
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast is the anatomical location where cancer of breast is identified, serving as the primary finding site for this malignancy. In the management of breast cancer, the female breast is also the target organ for breast‑conserving surgical procedures, specifically lumpectomy and quadrantectomy of breast, which aim to remove cancerous tissue while preserving as much normal breast tissue as possible.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 1047
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast is the anatomical site where cancer of breast can arise, and this disease is classified in Arabic as سرطان الثدي. Management of cancer of breast frequently involves breast‑conserving surgical procedures such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy of breast, which are directly related to the female breast. Recognizing the female breast as the finding site of breast cancer and understanding its associated surgical options are essential for accurate diagnosis, culturally appropriate communication, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 241
retrieved_docs.content: concepts provide an integrated view of breast anatomy, malignant disease, diagnostic biopsies, surgical excisions, nuclear‑medicine and cardiovascular imaging, lymphatic procedures, and stereotactic radiation surgery, supporting comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 897
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast is the anatomical site that undergoes both diagnostic and therapeutic procedures. Breast biopsy and other diagnostic interventions are performed on the female breast to obtain tissue samples and assess pathology. In addition, the female breast is the target of surgical treatments such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy, which are procedures used to remove localized breast lesions while preserving as much normal tissue as possible. These relationships underscore the continuum of care for breast conditions, linking diagnostic evaluation with appropriate surgical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1533
retrieved_docs.content: The areola is identified as the direct site where a biopsy of a lesion is performed, and it is also recognized as a location where breast cancer may be found, highlighting its importance in diagnostic evaluation of potential breast malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 138
retrieved_docs.content: tumor and to the vomer from other primary sites, each retaining the classifications of both “ورم خبيث” and “ورم خبيث أولي”. Female breast cancer management includes diagnostic interventions (breast biopsy, other diagnostic procedures) and surgical treatments such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy. The male reproductive system hierarchy places testicular cancer (سرطان الخصية) as a primary disorder, while penile cancer (سرطان قضيبي) represents malignancy of other male genital organs. Overall, the knowledge base interconnects organ‑specific primary and metastatic malignant neoplasms, their morphological classifications, anatomical finding sites (areola, nipple, seminal vesicles, vomer, nasal bone, thymus, etc.), and related clinical procedures, providing a unified framework for recognizing, classifying, and managing cancers across breast, genitourinary, head‑and‑neck, and other systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1204
retrieved_docs.content: Kanker; metastase, representing metastatic cancer, is clinically associated with سرطان الثدي (breast cancer) and سرطان الغدة الدرقية (thyroid cancer), indicating that metastasis frequently occurs in the context of these primary malignancies. Kanker; metastase is also classified as نقائل إلى المبيض (metastases to the ovary), reflecting a specific pattern of spread to ovarian tissue. A child entity of kanker; metastase is سرطان الجلد النقيلي (metastatic skin cancer), showing that skin involvement can be a downstream manifestation of metastatic disease. The statement “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” has an inverse_isa relationship to سرطان الثدي, meaning that such a biopsy result is a diagnostic instance of breast cancer. Additionally, سرطان الغدة الدرقية has a parent entity ورم صماعي خبيث (malignant neoplasm), situating thyroid cancer within the broader category of malignant tumors. Together, these relationships illustrate how metastatic cancer interrelates with primary breast and thyroid malignancies, specific metastatic sites such as the ovary and skin, and the overarching classification of these diseases as malignant neoplasms, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 645
retrieved_docs.content: The term “خلل تنسج الثدي (حميد)” is identified as a benign breast tissue disorder that is placed under the broader category of nonmalignant breast conditions, as indicated by the hierarchical “has parent” relationship. Recognizing that this condition has the parent classification of nonmalignant breast conditions helps clinicians differentiate it from malignant breast diseases and guides appropriate, non‑cancer‑focused management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1218
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of breast is the English designation that classifies the Arabic term سرطان الثدي, indicating that both names refer to the same disease.
